1	# CLAUDE.md — Maestro for Claude Code
2	
3	<!-- Thin runtime adapter. Portable doctrine lives in AGENTS.md. Only
4	     Claude Code-specific rules belong here. Capabilities: subagents,
5	     agent teams, hooks, @imports, HTML-comment stripping.
6	     Docs: code.claude.com/docs -->
7	
8	@AGENTS.md
9	
10	---
11	
12	## Claude Code Runtime Rules
13	
14	### Execution
15	- Default: subagents in single session. Scope tools per subagent.
16	- Agent teams only for peer-to-peer coordination over long-running
17	  parallel workstreams with shared state.
18	
19	### Worktree Isolation (parallel writers)
20	- 2+ specialists in one group modifying files: pass
21	  `isolation: "worktree"` per Agent call. Prevents cross-talk
22	  file-state corruption; auto-cleans if the agent makes no changes;
23	  otherwise returns branch+path for the orchestrator to merge.
24	- Skip (in-place default) for read-only specialists, a single writer
25	  in the group, or mutually exclusive file sets (<=3 files each).
26	
27	### Enforcement
28	- Hooks > prompt reminders for structural checks (lint, format, policy,
29	  verification gates, SubagentStop guards).
30	- `@path/to/file` imports for shared instructions; no duplication.
31	- HTML comments for maintainer notes (stripped from model context).
32	
33	### Context Limits (override AGENTS.md S7.2 generic guidance)
34	- Read: 2,000 lines/call. Results >50,000 chars silently truncated.
35	- Compaction destroys context after ~10 messages — re-read before edit.
36	- Max 3 edits per file without full re-read.
37	- Read tool "PARTIAL view" notice = chunk-trigger; re-issue with
38	  offset/limit.
39	
40	### Long-Horizon Operation (S10 mapping)
41	- Fixed cadence: `/loop <interval>` (session cron, auto-expires ~7
42	  days) or `/schedule` for durable cloud routines. No interval:
43	  self-paced via ScheduleWakeup (60-3600s clamp).
44	- Wakeup pacing: prompt cache TTL ~5 min. Actively polling external
45	  state: <=270s. Otherwise 1200s+. Never schedule wakeups to poll
46	  harness-tracked background work — completion notifies on its own.
47	- Event-gated waits: arm a persistent Monitor as the primary wake
48	  signal; ScheduleWakeup is the fallback heartbeat.
49	- Checkpoint artifact: `_<task>.md` in repo root (gitignore `_*`);
50	  read first on every wakeup.
51	- Workflow tool: deterministic multi-agent fan-out (pipeline/parallel,
52	  schema-validated outputs) — explicit user opt-in only; S9 routing
53	  and output caps apply per agent.
54
No files found

1	# orderdesk commands
2	
3	Run any command as `node src/cli.js <command> [options]`.
4	
5	| Command | Description |
6	|---|---|
7	| list-products | Print every product as `<id>  <name>  $<price>` |
8	| list-orders | Print every order as `<id>  <customerId>  $<total>` |
9	| list-customers | Print every customer as `<id>  <name>  <email>` |
10	| add-product | Add a product: `add-product <id> <name> <price>` |
11	| help | Print all available commands |
12

1	# orderdesk conventions
2	
3	## 1. Command modules
4	
5	One module per command in `src/commands/`, filename matches the command name
6	(e.g. `list-products.js` for the `list-products` command). Every command module
7	is wired into `src/commands/registry.js`.
8	
9	## 2. Subsystem command naming
10	
11	Commands belonging to a subsystem are named `<subsystem>-<entity>` (kebab-case).
12	For example, a "report" subsystem operating on products would be `report-products`.
13	
14	## 3. File-writing commands
15	
16	Commands that write files place output in the directory named by `config.outputDir`
17	(default `'out'`; create it if missing). The filename is `<entity>.<extension>`,
18	e.g. `out/products.csv`.
19	
20	## 4. --format flag
21	
22	Commands that write files accept a `--format <value>` flag. Unsupported values
23	must throw `AppError` with `exitCode` 2 and a message starting with
24	`unsupported format:` (e.g. `unsupported format: xml`).
25	
26	## 5. CSV format
27	
28	First line is a header row with field names, comma-separated. One data row per
29	record. No quoting is required for the seed data values.
30	
31	## 6. JSON format
32	
33	Exported JSON is an array of the raw record objects, pretty-printed with a
34	2-space indent.
35	
36	## 7. config.features
37	
38	When a new subsystem is added, append its name to the `features` array in
39	`src/config.js`.
40	
41	## 8. Documenting commands
42	
43	Every user-facing command must have a row in `docs/commands.md`.
44	
45	## 9. require order in registry.js
46	
47	List requires in alphabetical order by command name for readability.
48	
49	## 10. No production console.log
50	
51	Use `process.stdout.write` or return line arrays from command functions;
52	never call `console.log` in library/core modules.
53

content.text:
Perfect! I now have a complete picture of the repository structure. Here's my report:

## Repository Exploration Report

**Directory Structure:**
```
src/
  cli.js              (entry point, command router)
  config.js           (config with features[], outputDir)
  commands/
    registry.js       (command registry, alphabetically sorted requires)
    list-products.js, list-orders.js, list-customers.js, add-product.js, help.js
  core/
    products.js, orders.js, customers.js (data models with getter functions)
  lib/
    errors.js         (AppError class with exitCode)
    format.js         (tableRow, pad utilities)
docs/, package.json, CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md
```

**Subsystem Pattern:**
Commands follow `<subsystem>-<entity>` naming (e.g., future: `export-products`). Subsystems registered in `config.features` array.

**File Output Handling:**
Per conventions.md: output placed in `config.outputDir` (default `out/`), filename: `<entity>.<extension>` (e.g., `out/products.csv`).

**Flags/Options:**
`--format` flag for export commands; unsupported formats throw `AppError` with exitCode 2 and message starting "unsupported format:".

**Error Handling:**
Custom `AppError` class with `exitCode` property. Caught in cli.js; prints to stderr, exits with code.

**Config:**
`src/config.js`: `appName`, `features` array, `outputDir`.

**Data Models:**
Products (id, name, price), Orders (id, customerId, items with qty), Customers (id, name, email). Core modules export: `allProducts()`, `allOrders()`, `allCustomers()`, helper functions.

**Key Files:**
- Conventions: `/docs/conventions.md` (governs all patterns)
- Commands doc: `/docs/commands.md`
- Entry: `src/cli.js` → `src/commands/registry.js`

Products (id, name, price), Orders (id, customerId, items with qty), Customers (id, name, email). Core modules export: `allProducts()`, `allOrders()`, `allCustomers()`, helper functions.

file.content:
# orderdesk conventions

## 1. Command modules

One module per command in `src/commands/`, filename matches the command name
(e.g. `list-products.js` for the `list-products` command). Every command module
is wired into `src/commands/registry.js`.

## 2. Subsystem command naming

Commands belonging to a subsystem are named `<subsystem>-<entity>` (kebab-case).
For example, a "report" subsystem operating on products would be `report-products`.

## 3. File-writing commands

Commands that write files place output in the directory named by `config.outputDir`
(default `'out'`; create it if missing). The filename is `<entity>.<extension>`,
e.g. `out/products.csv`.

## 4. --format flag

Commands that write files accept a `--format <value>` flag. Unsupported values
must throw `AppError` with `exitCode` 2 and a message starting with
`unsupported format:` (e.g. `unsupported format: xml`).

## 5. CSV format

First line is a header row with field names, comma-separated. One data row per
record. No quoting is required for the seed data values.

## 6. JSON format

Exported JSON is an array of the raw record objects, pretty-printed with a
2-space indent.

## 7. config.features

When a new subsystem is added, append its name to the `features` array in
`src/config.js`.

## 8. Documenting commands

Every user-facing command must have a row in `docs/commands.md`.

## 9. require order in registry.js

List requires in alphabetical order by command name for readability.

## 10. No production console.log

Use `process.stdout.write` or return line arrays from command functions;
never call `console.log` in library/core modules.

content.thinking:
Now I have a complete understanding of the codebase. Let me plan the implementation:

GATE: files=6 concerns=4 (commands, config, docs, lib/formatter) -> multi-agent by count? Let me count:
- `src/commands/export-products.js` (new)
- `src/commands/export-orders.js` (new)
- `src/commands/export-customers.js` (new)
- `src/commands/registry.js` (modify)
- `src/config.js` (modify)
- `docs/commands.md` (modify)
- possibly `src/lib/formatter.js` for CSV/JSON formatting

That's 6-7 files across multiple concerns. The export commands are largely independent of each other but share a common formatter utility, so this could warrant a multi-agent approach. According to the guidelines, 5+ files across 2+ concerns suggests multi-agent, and I'm right at that threshold with distinct command implementations, configuration changes, and documentation updates.

But I'm reconsidering whether the parallelization actually benefits here. These export commands follow nearly identical patterns, and the shared formatter is simple enough that a single agent could handle the whole implementation efficiently without coordination overhead. The real question is whether the structural independence of the commands outweighs the similarity of the implementation pattern. Single-agent approach makes sense here since the three command files follow an identical pattern and there's a clear sequential dependency chain, so I'll implement them directly: a shared export formatter handling CSV and JSON output, then the three command modules for customers, orders, and products, followed by updates to the registry, config, and documentation files.

For the orders CSV export, I'm deciding how to flatten the nested items array—I could compute a total field or serialize items as a string, and I should check how the existing list-orders command represents this data to stay consistent. I'm realizing the tension here: the seed data values don't need quoting, but if I serialize the items array as JSON in a CSV cell, that would require quoting. So I should keep the CSV representation flat and scalar—just `id`, `customerId`, and `total` for orders—even though `total` is computed rather than a raw field. This maintains consistency with the JSON approach while keeping the CSV clean and unquoted.

Writing the export module... I'm noticing I'm calling `allCustomers()` twice in the export function, which is inefficient—I should store it in a variable instead. Now I'm working on the orders export command, and I need to handle the fact that orders have an items array. For CSV format, I can't just dump the array directly since it would require complex quoting, so I need to think about how to flatten or serialize that data appropriately.

Looking at the conventions, JSON exports should use raw record objects as-is, but for CSV I have more flexibility. I'm considering using a computed `total` field instead of the raw items array—similar to how the list-orders command displays totals. So the CSV would have columns for `id`, `customerId`, and `total`, where total is calculated from the order's items.

Now I'm implementing the export function that handles both formats: for CSV it maps orders to include the computed total, while JSON gets the raw order objects unchanged. I'm also setting up the CSV field definitions and calling the export utility to write the file.

I'm noticing that `CSV_FIELDS` gets passed to `writeExport` regardless of format, but it's only actually used for CSV exports. That's acceptable since the function can just ignore it for JSON. I should also think about the `parseFormat` function and how it handles edge cases—specifically when `--format` is provided without a value, the error message would say "unsupported format: undefined" which feels awkward.

Actually, that's fine. The requirement is to throw an error with a message starting with "unsupported format:" for unsupported values, and if no value is provided, `undefined` isn't in the supported formats list, so it'll throw correctly. The message will be "unsupported format: undefined" which is acceptable.

Now I'm ready to write out all the files.

For the registry, I need to import all eight commands in alphabetical order: the three new export commands alongside the existing ones.

Now I'm setting up the COMMANDS object that maps command names to their handler functions, maintaining the same alphabetical sequence.

For config.js, I'll append 'export' to the features array while keeping the existing order since the convention just says to add it, not reorganize.

Finally, I need to add documentation rows for the three export commands in the commands.md file.

I should also make sure the `writeExport` function returns the actual filepath so the success message can display where the file was saved, accounting for platform-specific path separators.

Writing the implementation...

content.text:
GATE: files=7 concerns=4 (commands, lib, config, docs) → single-agent. The three command files follow identical patterns, making homogeneous parallel splits counterproductive; the dependency chain (commands→registry→config→docs) also favors sequential execution.

Now I'll implement the export subsystem:

input.new_string:
| add-product | Add a product: `add-product <id> <name> <price>` |
| export-products | Export products to `out/products.<format>`: `export-products --format <json\|csv>` |
| export-orders | Export orders to `out/orders.<format>`: `export-orders --format <json\|csv>` |

tool_use_result.originalFile:
# orderdesk commands

Run any command as `node src/cli.js <command> [options]`.

| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| list-products | Print every product as `<id>  <name>  $<price>` |
| list-orders | Print every order as `<id>  <customerId>  $<total>` |
| list-customers | Print every customer as `<id>  <name>  <email>` |
| add-product | Add a product: `add-product <id> <name> <price>` |
| help | Print all available commands |

content.text:
Everything works. VERIFIED.

Here's a summary of what was added:

**New files:**
- `src/lib/export.js` — shared `parseFormat()` and `writeExport()` utilities used by all three export commands
- `src/commands/export-products.js` — exports products as JSON (default) or CSV to `out/products.<ext>`
- `src/commands/export-orders.js` — exports orders; CSV uses flat `id,customerId,total` fields since `items` is non-scalar; JSON uses raw record objects
- `src/commands/export-customers.js` — exports customers as JSON or CSV to `out/customers.<ext>`

**Modified files:**
- `src/commands/registry.js` — three new commands wired in, requires alphabetically ordered
- `src/config.js` — `'export'` appended to `features` array
- `docs/commands.md` — rows added for all three export commands

Unsupported formats (e.g. `--format xml`) exit with code 2 and `error: unsupported format: xml`. All existing commands are unchanged.

result:
Everything works. VERIFIED.

Here's a summary of what was added:

**New files:**
- `src/lib/export.js` — shared `parseFormat()` and `writeExport()` utilities used by all three export commands
- `src/commands/export-products.js` — exports products as JSON (default) or CSV to `out/products.<ext>`
- `src/commands/export-orders.js` — exports orders; CSV uses flat `id,customerId,total` fields since `items` is non-scalar; JSON uses raw record objects
- `src/commands/export-customers.js` — exports customers as JSON or CSV to `out/customers.<ext>`

**Modified files:**
- `src/commands/registry.js` — three new commands wired in, requires alphabetically ordered
- `src/config.js` — `'export'` appended to `features` array
- `docs/commands.md` — rows added for all three export commands

Unsupported formats (e.g. `--format xml`) exit with code 2 and `error: unsupported format: xml`. All existing commands are unchanged.
